generated_oml:
instance SiemensSinumerik840DSLRobot : DTDFVocab:PhysicalTwinSystem [
    base:desc "The Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl-controlled serial robot MABI MAX 100 focusing on high-precision motion control for industrial manufacturing automation, specifically its mechanical and kinematic components"
]

instance FirstAxisMechanicalComponents : DTDFVocab:PhysicalComponent [
    base:desc "Mechanical components of the first axis including motor (servo motor), gearboxes, and articulated arm structure for high-precision motion control"
]

instance SiemensSinumerik840DslPLC : DTDFVocab:PhysicalComponent [
    base:desc "Direct integration with Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl PLC for real-time motion coordination and closed-loop positioning control"
]

instance PermanentMagnetSynchronousMotor : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision permanent magnet synchronous motor with embedded encoder feedback driving the first axis of the robot"
]

instance GearboxSystem : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Gearbox assembly for power transmission and mechanical linkage in the robot's first axis"
]

instance ArticulatedArmStructure : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Mechanical linkages forming the articulated arm structure for the first axis of the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl robot"
]

instance FirstAxisEncoder1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft for angular displacement measurement of the first axis"
    DTDFVocab:producedData FirstAxisEncoderDataStream
]

instance FirstAxisEncoder2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Second position encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft for redundancy and high-resolution angular displacement measurement of the first axis"
    DTDFVocab:producedData FirstAxisEncoderDataStream
]

instance KinematicErrorSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor arrangement measuring kinematic errors and positional uncertainties for motion accuracy optimization"
    DTDFVocab:producedData KinematicErrorDataStream
]

instance FirstAxisEncoderDataStream : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom FirstAxisEncoder1, FirstAxisEncoder2
    base:desc "High-frequency angular displacement data streams from dual encoders for real-time kinematic monitoring"
]

instance KinematicErrorDataStream : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom KinematicErrorSensor
    base:desc "Contextual kinematic error data streams for friction identification and motion accuracy optimization"
]

instance AngularPositionData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData FirstAxisEncoderDataStream
    base:desc "High-resolution angular position data traces aggregated from dual encoder measurements for digital twin context"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowAggregator
]

instance KinematicErrorTraces : DTDFVocab:Data [
    DTDFVocab:fromData KinematicErrorDataStream
    base:desc "Contextual kinematic error traces for friction modeling and motion accuracy validation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowAggregator
]

instance StateTraces : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Comprehensive state traces including operational parameters and environmental factors for manufacturing automation context"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowAggregator
]

instance DigitalShadowAggregator : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Automated passive aggregation system that collects and abstracts contextual data traces from physical sensors for digital twin relevance"
]

instance DataPreprocessingFilter : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Contextual data abstraction and preprocessing component that aligns physical data with digital modeling requirements"
]

instance MechanicalKinematicModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Geometric and kinematic model of the robot's first axis components derived from AADL/SysML architecture descriptions"
]

instance FrictionModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based friction model using Coulomb-Viscous-Stribeck equations for multi-physics-aware analysis"
]

instance MotionAccuracyModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model capturing motion accuracy optimization requirements and kinematic error compensation"
]

instance SimulinkBehavioralModel : DTDFVocab:SimulationTool [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for dynamic behavior simulation of robot motion and control systems"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionModelSimulation, MotionAccuracySimulation
]

instance PhysicsSimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:SimulationTool [
    base:desc "General-purpose physics simulation engine for validating mechanical and kinematic models"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MechanicalKinematicModel, FrictionModel
]

instance MotionMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time monitoring service that aggregates and analyzes contextual data traces for operational state assessment"
    DTDFVocab:provides MotionStateInsights
]

instance FrictionAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Data-driven service for friction identification and predictive maintenance through contextual data analysis"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionModelInsights
]

instance OptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Data-driven optimization service for improving motion accuracy and control system performance"
    DTDFVocab:provides ControlOptimizationActions
]

instance MotionStateInsights : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time insights into robot motion state including positional accuracy, kinematic errors, and operational context"
]

instance FrictionModelInsights : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predictive analytics results from friction models showing wear patterns, thermal dynamics, and maintenance recommendations"
]

instance ControlOptimizationActions : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated control adjustments for improving motion accuracy through friction compensation and error correction"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance RobotFirstAxisEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains SiemensSinumerik840DSLRobot, FirstAxisMechanicalComponents
]

instance ManufacturingAutomationContext : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains MotionMonitoringService, FrictionAnalysisService, OptimizationService
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system in the provided context operates with a **real-time, data-driven representation** of the physical system, where temporal aspects are implicitly tied to the **live data aggregation** for a specific purpose. While the documents do not specify explicit synchronization frequencies (e.g., Hz, ms), they emphasize that the digital shadow collects **contextual data traces aggregated over discrete time intervals** to maintain alignment with the physical system. The temporal granularity is implied by the requirement for **continuous or near-real-time updates** to ensure accurate state representation, though no precise latency or sampling rate is detailed. The system’s behavior is modeled at multiple abstraction levels (e.g., requirements, functional architecture), where time-sensitive models (e.g., state machines in SysML) may enforce constraints on timing behavior during simulation or execution phases."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "A Digital Twin system in this context is structured to leverage **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, particularly through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML IBDs**, which define hierarchical component and connector models. The system integrates **behavioral models** (e.g., MontiArc Automata, SysML state machines) alongside simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (C++, Java) to ensure synchronized temporal behavior across physical and digital components. The **Digital Shadow** serves as a foundational passive data aggregation layer, characterized by a **one-way automated data flow** from physical objects to digital representations, while the **Digital Twin** introduces active processing components to interpret and act upon this data. The orchestration of these elements is implicitly implied by the need for **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-perspective modeling**, though explicit synchronization frequencies, granularity, or latency requirements are not detailed in the provided documents."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described context spans **multi-layered abstraction stages** aligned with Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies, structured through distinct phases:
1. **Requirements Phase**: Defines functional and non-functional specifications using **SysML** or **ADLs** (e.g., AADL, ACME) to capture system needs.
2. **Functional/Logical Architecture Phase**: Models components, connectors, and interconnections via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **MontiArc** or **SysML IBDs**, supplemented by **state machines** or **automata** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton) for behavior.
3. **Simulation/Implementation Phase**: Integrates models with tools such as **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation frameworks (e.g., [29], [30]), or general-purpose languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**) to validate system behavior.
**Transition Management**: The lifecycle transitions are governed by **methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**, ensuring alignment across abstraction layers. The **digital shadow** serves as a foundational context for aggregation and abstraction of data traces (e.g., Kritzinger et al. [32]), enabling phased representation shifts.
**Lifecycle Governance**: Heterogeneous stakeholders’ perspectives are integrated via **cross-tool and notation compatibility**, ensuring consistency across **SysML**, **ADLs**, and simulation environments. No explicit lifecycle governance framework (e.g., CMMI for DTs) is mentioned, but MBSE principles implicitly govern transitions.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
  Fidelity and validity are ensured through:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**: Multi-physics-aware models (e.g., friction identification) align with physical constraints via MBSE validation.
  - **Validation/Verification Methods**: Direct comparison of **reference values** (ground truth) with **simulated values** (DT models) to detect discrepancies.
  - **Uncertainty Sources**: Mitigated via sensor arrangement (e.g., dual encoders) and backlash reduction techniques.
  - **Error Detection/Correction**: Continuous feedback loops between physical and digital domains, leveraging active DT components to auto-correct deviations.
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**: Implicit in MBSE methodologies (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) for cross-stakeholder consistency and interoperability.
  The DT system prioritizes real-time validation to maintain operational integrity, though specific thresholds (e.g., error margins) are not detailed.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
  The **Digital Twin (DT)** infrastructure, while not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, is implied to require:
  - **Active components**: To enable bidirectional interaction (unlike digital shadows), DTs incorporate software systems capable of interpreting, processing, and executing commands autonomously (e.g., state machines, MontiArc Automata).
  - **Technical network infrastructure**: The documents reference **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs) and tools like **MathWorks Simulink** for behavior modeling, suggesting that DTs rely on **heterogeneous communication protocols** (e.g., SysML state machines, MontiArc Automata) to bridge physical-digital gaps.
  - **No explicit mention of protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), topology (e.g., star, mesh), bandwidth/latency requirements, or redundancy mechanisms**: The documents focus on **passive data aggregation** (digital shadows) and **modeling languages** (SysML, ADLs) but do not specify DT-specific connectivity details.
  - **Security protocols**: Not addressed; the documents do not reference encryption, authentication (e.g., TLS, OAuth), or network security frameworks for DTs.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
  The document does not provide details on hosting infrastructure (cloud/edge/on-premise) or deployment models. However, the following **modeling and simulation tools** are mentioned for DT development:
  - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**: Uses **SysML** for architecture description and **MathWorks Simulink** for behavior modeling.
  - **General-purpose languages**: **C++/Java** for implementing system logic or simulation code.
  - **Multi-stage modeling**: Systems are decomposed into abstraction layers (e.g., RFLP: Requirements → Functions → Logics → Physics) across heterogeneous tools.
  No mention of **scalability (e.g., Kubernetes, CI/CD)**, **storage requirements (e.g., HPC clusters)**, or **availability guarantees** (e.g., redundancy, failover) is provided.
  The deployment context remains abstract, focusing on **modeling methodologies** rather than operational infrastructure.
  "
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
  The Digital Twin system adheres to the following **industry-specific standards and compliance frameworks**:
  1. **Modeling Languages and Tools**:
     - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**: Uses **SysML** (Interface Block Diagrams, SysML v1.7 state machines) for architecture description and behavior modeling.
     - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Employs **AADL** (for real-time systems) and **MontiArc** (for component/connector modeling).
     - **Simulation Tools**: Integrates **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools for model validation.
  2. **Standards Compliance**:
     - **Industry-Specific Frameworks**: Follows **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) for lifecycle governance, though no explicit mention of **ISO/IEC/IEEE 20969** (SysML standard) is provided in the documents.
     - **Interoperability**: Ensures compatibility via **ADLs** (e.g., AADL, MontiArc) and **SysML IBDs**, enabling seamless integration of heterogeneous components.
  3. **Certification and Compliance**:
     - **Tool-Specific Standards**: Compliance with tool-specific standards (e.g., SysML, AADL) for validation and certification.
     - **Data Trace Standards**: Adheres to **digital shadow paradigms** (automated one-way data flows) for data aggregation and abstraction.
     - **No Explicit Certification Requirements**: The documents do not specify certification bodies or compliance frameworks for the Digital Twin system.
  "
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework (as defined in [Plattform Industrie 4.0, 2019](50)) to enforce structured safety and security protocols within the industrial automation pyramid. Specifically, the system integrates a **safety cell** for the MABI robotic arm (Model: Max-100 2-25-P, [MABI, 2025](44)), which restricts the robot's operational workspace to predefined boundaries to mitigate collision risks with human operators or other machinery. This approach aligns with industrial safety standards implicitly by ensuring the robot operates within a physically constrained zone, reducing unintended movements.

The Digital Twin employs **model-driven engineering** (as outlined in [Heithoff et al., 2025](43)) to dynamically simulate and validate safety-critical behaviors, ensuring compliance with real-time operational constraints. While the documents do not explicitly mention explicit cybersecurity protocols (e.g., encryption standards, access control mechanisms), the AAS architecture inherently supports **semantic interoperability** (as referenced in [Bader & Maleshkova, 2019](46) and [Wei et al., 2019](47)) for secure data exchange between the physical and digital domains. The system’s reliance on standardized ontologies and knowledge graphs ensures that safety-critical data integrity is preserved during transmission and processing.

No explicit mention of fail-safe mechanisms, risk assessment frameworks (e.g., HAZOP), or compliance with standards like **ISO/SAE 21434** is documented in the provided context. However, the integration of the AAS with the **Industrie 4.0 Reference Architecture Model (RAMI 4.0)**—though not directly cited—implies adherence to foundational safety and security principles across the automation layers (as per [DIN IÖB, 2016](49) and [Frysak et al., 2018](48)). The system’s operational scope is limited to the robot’s physical constraints and digital twin’s simulation fidelity, without detailed cybersecurity protocols or compliance certifications."
]
